title: Elliptic curves of rank two and generalised Kato classes
speaker: Francesc Castella
abstract: The generalised Kato classes of Darmon-Rotger arise as $p$-adic limits of diagonal cycles on triple products of modular curves, and in some cases, they are predicted to have a bearing on the arithmetic of elliptic curves over $Q$ of rank two. In this talk, we will report on a joint work in progress with Ming-Lun Hsieh concerning a special case of the conjectures of Darmon-Rotger.

proof_stages.label: Dihedral Hida-family setup
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes an elliptic curve E/Q, an ordinary prime p, an imaginary quadratic field K satisfying a Heegner-type sign condition, and weight-one theta series g and h placed in ordinary Hida families.

proof_stages.label: Darmon–Rotger reciprocity and generalized Kato classes
proof_stages.summary: A special case of Darmon–Rotger is recalled: a triple-product p-adic L-function, a big global cohomology class, and a big logarithm map are related by an explicit reciprocity law, whose weight-one specialization defines generalized Kato classes and gives a Selmer criterion via vanishing of the central value.

proof_stages.label: Rank-two specialization of the Darmon–Rotger conjecture
proof_stages.summary: In the theta-series setting the triple tensor representation and triple-product L-function split into elliptic-curve factors, allowing the generalized Kato classes to be projected to H^1(Q,V_pE) and the rank-two Darmon–Rotger conjecture to be stated as a predicted nonzero p-adic line, namely the kernel of the p-adic logarithm.

proof_stages.label: The Hsieh theorem via the anticyclotomic theta element
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem with Ming-Lun Hsieh is stated: under residual irreducibility and ramification hypotheses, if the anticyclotomic theta element vanishes to order exactly two at the trivial character, then the p-adic Selmer group has dimension two and the Darmon–Rotger formulas for the four generalized Kato classes hold.

proof_stages.label: First computation from the anticyclotomic main conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy begins by computing the second derivative of the anticyclotomic theta element using the anticyclotomic main conjecture, a control theorem for the Iwasawa module, and derived regulators built from the degenerate anticyclotomic p-adic height.

proof_stages.label: Rank split and derived-height leading term
proof_stages.summary: Under the order-two hypothesis the control-theoretic inequality and nonvanishing force the plus/minus Selmer ranks over K to be 2 and 0, so the nullspace of the first height is the whole Selmer group and the second derivative is expressed, up to a p-adic unit, as the square of a derived height pairing on a basis P,Q.

proof_stages.label: Rubin height formula and comparison
proof_stages.summary: A comparison of the triple-product p-adic L-function with the anticyclotomic theta element, together with a Lubin–Tate variant of Rubin's height formula applied to the Darmon–Rotger big class, gives a second formula involving the generalized Kato classes, and comparing the two formulas yields the predicted expressions.

examples.name: Weight-two diagonal cycle on modular curves
examples.note: The speaker makes the interpolation of the big cohomology class concrete by noting that, in weight two, the diagonal cycles come from the diagonal embedding of a modular curve into a triple product of modular curves; this illustrates an already stated construction rather than generating a new idea.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker only identifies the weight-two diagonal cycles as coming from the diagonal embedding of a modular curve into a triple product, without computing or working through that cycle.

passes.reason: It is the lowest-weight/base specialization where the generalized Kuga–Sato construction collapses to the ordinary diagonal in a triple product of modular curves, used only illustratively.
It is the minimal weight-two/base geometric case of the diagonal-cycle construction, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.

The weight-two diagonal cycle is the minimal geometric-weight base case of the diagonal-cycle family, sitting at the lower boundary and carrying the full factor-permutation symmetry of the diagonal in a triple product.

The weight-two diagonal cycle is the lowest geometric-weight/base case, realized by the fully factor-symmetric diagonal embedding of a modular curve into its triple product, and is used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
the diagonal cycle is permutation-symmetric in the triple product and weight two is the minimal geometric base case before the higher-weight Kuga–Sato construction
The weight-two cycle is the canonical diagonal embedding of a modular curve in a triple product, giving a highly regular symmetric illustrative specialization.
It is used illustratively as the weight-two specialization, and the diagonal cycle is structurally symmetric under permutation of the product factors.

Used only as an illustrative specialization; the weight-two diagonal is the minimal boundary case of the higher-weight diagonal-cycle construction and is symmetric under permutation of the three factors.
The weight-two diagonal cycle is used constructively as the geometric specialization of the big cohomology class, and the diagonal in a triple product is a highly regular symmetric cycle.
The weight-two diagonal cycle is used illustratively as the modular-curve diagonal specialization, and the small diagonal is a highly symmetric standard cycle.
the small diagonal is permutation-symmetric and the weight-two modular-curve case is the minimal/base geometric specialization, used illustratively rather than adversarially
A weight-two diagonal cycle is the standard diagonal embedding of a modular curve in a triple product, used illustratively for the interpolation formula and not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Cyclotomic Iwasawa contrast
examples.note: The cyclotomic setting is used as a comparison where the p-adic height is expected to be nondegenerate and Rubin's original formula applies, clarifying why the anticyclotomic proof needs derived heights and a variant of Rubin's formula.
examples.engagement_reason: The cyclotomic Iwasawa case is invoked only as a contrast where the p-adic height is expected nondegenerate and Rubin’s original formula applies, with no concrete derivation in that setting.

passes.reason: The cyclotomic Iwasawa setting is invoked as the standard nondegenerate comparison with the anticyclotomic case, not as a refuting example.
The cyclotomic Iwasawa setting is a standard comparison case with expected nondegenerate height pairing, not a structural exception or a counterexample here.
The cyclotomic Iwasawa setting is used only as a standard comparison case with nondegenerate expected heights, not to refute a stated claim.
The cyclotomic Iwasawa case is a standard comparison setting where the height is expected nondegenerate, invoked as a contrast rather than as a counterexample.
the cyclotomic Iwasawa setting is a standard systematic comparison case used only to contrast expected nondegeneracy with the anticyclotomic situation
The cyclotomic Iwasawa setting is a standard systematic comparison case, used for contrast rather than to refute a claim.
The cyclotomic Iwasawa case is used as a standard comparison where the height is expected nondegenerate, not as a refuting or structurally sporadic example.
Used as a comparison with the anticyclotomic setting; the cyclotomic Iwasawa case is a standard systematic case with expected nondegenerate height.

The cyclotomic Iwasawa case is invoked only as a comparative baseline where the height is expected nondegenerate, not as a refutation, and it is a standard systematic case rather than structurally special.
The cyclotomic Iwasawa case is invoked only as a standard contrast where the expected height is nondegenerate, not as a structural exception or counterexample.
the ordinary cyclotomic Iwasawa setting is invoked only as a contrast where the height is expected nondegenerate, not as a structural exception or refutation
